item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first two images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image is composed of copyrighted images from here and the second image is composed of various images whose sources show no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the images have been removed. The last image was made for the article and thus is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Museum in background in first picture and bar jacks in the "freshwater" picture in the collapsible killed it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This uses a lot of words for not a whole lot of payoff. Like, I think the gist [SPOILER ALERT] is that these things are supposedly the next stage of human evolution, and also predating humanity? But there's absolutely nothing backing that up at all, depriving it of any impact whatsoever. And there's another half-hearted "oh you're afraid of different things you're so arrogant you suuuuck" jab at the Foundation that, frankly, I'm not interested in any more (really, there are good criticisms to be made of the Foundation, stick with those).

Also, what the hell is "a cambium market variation"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
English translation for "прогресс инженерной"

Seems out of context. This translates as "progress of something feminine and engineering-related", for example, "прогресс инженерной науки" - "the progress of engineering science".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why people don't see this more often. Maybe it's just me, and I know I'm easily impressed by aesthetics, but I'm digging this one. Quick shift from benign to downright horrid, then (as I realize that this isn't gonna be some nightmare fuel) back to just kinda off. Perhaps if it had been more of a whiplash over to pure horror, it would have appealed to me more but, for now, nice job.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When approached by any sentient organism within a ten kilometer radius, SCP-2595-D will undergo a spontaneous modification.

Supplementary analysis of the phenomena taking place on the structures of SCP-2595-D has unveiled that the creation of a new instance is concurrent with the disappearance of adult human beings located around the area of the ██████ Reservoir.

So is it the person activating it that disappears or just some random person in the area? The "supplementary analysis" bit implies that they only realized people were disappearing later, after they initially discovered the anomaly, and the person who triggers it is left alone while someone else disappears. If that's the case…it's a bit of an odd effect. It'd be like triggering a burglar alarm in someone's house and the police raid a house down the street and arrest the owner, or someone triggering a motion-activated camera that takes a picture of someone else.

That being said, the collapsible is…meh? It went from "nomadic animate polymer sculptures" to "You're messing with things you don't understand and we're better than you. DOOM! DOOOOOOOOOM!" On the whole, it isn't a downvote, but the collapsible puts it south of neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand why the collapsible is needed? There doesn't seem to me anything particularly secret about the containment parameters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, the whole problem was it's misspelled in the article! Geez. And you changed it anyway, so okay!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the first news article, in the collapsible, the text reads, "…showcasing a jaw-breaking display of limestone carving skills…". Should this be 'jaw-dropping'? If not, it's an expression that I've never heard of in this context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really feeling this one. Maybe I'm missing something, but -A through -C don't really seem that connected to -D, and the whole "we are eternal and you are arrogant" speech seemed a bi cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it should, and it has been changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has been changed to imply that only human beings are affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I think it's definitely a word, but yeah, it's kind of weird to have it in a research document. "Colonial multicellular organisms" works perfectly fine and doesn't raise any eyebrows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It basically means 'colonial' or multicellular organisms that form communities. Tie that into the context of the article, and it makes a sense in an offhand sort of way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pluricellular =/= multicellular.
They refer to entirely different things. Multicellular organisms are humans, plants, and fungi, but pluricellular organisms are slime molds and such. Using pluricellular in this case implies it doesn't work on multicellular organisms, which would include humans.
Furthermore, pluricellular is very rarely used, and is the kind of thing you might not see even on a scientific document. Apparently there's actually a pretty good argument that it's not even a word, as it's too niche to have a place in even a dictionary and the vast majority of even Google results turn up very little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except that I can't find a definition for it. What does it mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe modify it to make it clearer that the effect works on people, instead of just pluricellular organisms. (And shouldn't it be multicellular?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried to make that clear in this paragraph, right before the interview.

Additionally, it has been recognized by further observation that the creation of new human-shaped structures within SCP-2595-D coincides directly with the death of a senior member of the society designated as SCP-2595 and the following birth of a new component holding several characteristics that resemble the newly created limestone formation, including body shape, facial complexion and dressing amenities.

Is there anything that causes this ambiguity you guys are describing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How you described 2595-D's function is not what I got from the description at all. In fact, I thought it worked the way Light described it, before guessing from the interview log that apparently this does something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did miss it, and i'll be happy to answer.

click to show
hide

How long do we think the figures have been around?

I decided not to specify the time of the "creation" of figures due to two main reasons:

  • The article was getting way too big and i did not think a recovery log was necessary.
  • The rewording on the interview implies that the entities have been inhabiting the planet for a long time.

What's with the giant black box?

It's supposed to represent a log-in console. I like to use CSS and i tried to add a feeling of immersion by using the ListUsers code subtly.

What's with the new object listing for SCP-XXXX-D? I don't think I've seen that before. In fact, why is it redacted or separate at all? Their separate containment procedures can go with the rest of the procedures specifying XXXX-D.

Because it involves a lot more than the main part of the article. Due to the existence of 2595-D, the Foundation eventually had to submerge a small down. And, also, the new object listing was in order in my opinion due to the difference in nature of the the entities. A very similar format is used on SCP-1968.

Why did the original containment of SCP-XXXX-D fail? (Submerging a town is, presumably, a lot of work. Why did they think that was the best option?)

The original draft that you read did not contain the last Addendum (the newspaper page). Take a look.

The statues basically started to materialize away from the main group of statues.

Can you clarify what's going on with SCP-XXXX-D? As I understand it: Person walks near SCP-XXXX-D. This destroys one SCP-XXXX-D, immediately makes another one just like it, also destroys an SCP-XXXX, and makes one just like it. Is there a reason you chose that?

  1. A person breaks the perimeter.
  2. The person disappears and the oldest instance of -D is destroyed.
  3. A new instance of -D resembling the person appears.
  4. Simultaneously, an entity similar to the disappeared person is born in SCP-2595 and the oldest member of SCP-2595 dies.

Is SCP-XXXX-339 also one of SCP-XXXX-D? If not, what is it, and how did it "submit itself for interrogation"?

No. SCP-2595-399 is a member of SCP-2595. The statues composing SCP-2595-D are not sentient or sapient. They are just statues that are directly connected to SCP-2595 (the sentient/sapient ones).

It submitted itself for interrogation after the Foundation attempted to cease the activity of SCP-2595-D by sinking it. The submission occurred "easily" due to the active presence of Foundation personnel within the community.


Collapsed the wall of text. ~Zyn

I hope this clarifies everything a little bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In case you missed it, I gave you some critique in the forums just before you posted this. I think most of it still works for this version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The final addendum implies the anomaly (specifically the area where people are snatched up) is growing and there's currently no way to stop it, so it's Keter.

Also, pluricellular works. It would make more sense than multicellular in the context of the article, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall interesting. Putting a second layer to the initial object was a good idea. Not sure why it's Keter necessarily. Also, I think you mean "multicellular" instead of "pluricelular" (I could only find non-English words when Googling that) and "jaw-dropping" instead of "jaw-breaking" in the first article image. Not sure how easy that will be to change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Grammar and spelling mistakes everywhere.
I also found everything under the collapsible difficult to parse. Also, the whole society is… strange. There were a few good ideas, but the whole thing doesn't add up very well. A matriarchal society that worships a patriarchal deity? They replace statues- but why? I think they're saying they're the next step in humanity, but why aren't they spreading the goodness? The idea is okay, but there are a lot of details that just don't add up.

E: Downvote. The spelling/word choice/grammar are actually interfering with comprehensibility, which is very not good and also bad. Better, but there are still a few edits that need to happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral negative. I don't really dig plastic people with the womens ruling everything following a patriarchal religion and also claiming to be next gen evolution in humanity or something. This could've gone in a much more interesting manner to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new interview is much better. Nice work. -1 becomes +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the "I" for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have added more content to the collapsible in order to minimize confusion and clarify the Keter-level given to the entity. Take a look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the pre-collapsible stuff, but the stuff under the collapsible is somewhat confusing to me. And that interview is… not so good.

No vote at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Keter-level is pretty much resumed in this small part.

with the expanding radius of the entity

The radius expands on a timely basis, and you can see that by the revisions of the procedures. It was originally affecting a neighborhood, then it started to affect a whole city, and then it started to affect monitoring personnel. It might eventually grasp a whole continent and you can't really contain it without evacuation measures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have made some changes to the interview based on general feedback and to correct some grammar errors. Some of the wording has been changed as well, to make the tone better and clearer.

And yes, the Task Force was indeed a typo from the restoration of an early draft. It has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was indeed a typo in the first paragraph and i have corrected it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote.

My main problem is style. Throughout the article, the grammar and word choice seem a little… off. Shaprtoe pointed out a lot of it, but in general some of the choices of technical terms, descriptions, and adjectives were possibly incorrect and a bit hard to follow.

(What I could say is, it feels translated. If it was translated, it came across rather well for the most part, but there are still some issues in the home stretch.)

This wasn't a real problem until the collapsible. I completely did not grasp the Keter-level ability of -D you described here in the comments, and the interview made no sense without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that's why I said it wasn't affecting my vote. I always thought of it as more of an in-joke than anything.

Also, I thought that the lowercase "i" was a style choice for 399 only (which would be tied to why the Foundation would transcribe it as such in universe), but the researcher doing it too makes me think it's just an error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Foundation shells typically end up with the acronym SCP, and those in other languages either have that acronym or take it on when translated into English. I don't know if this is enforced or just what most authors default to as an in-joke, but I figured I'd bring it up. It isn't affecting my vote.

It's definitely not a necessary thing. Some people specifically frown on it I've heard tell.

Regarding the "i"s, if it's deliberate then there's still a disconnect. It's a transcript of a spoken interview; there has to be some understanding of why:

  • the Foundation would know, from hearing it, that it was intended to be lowercased.
  • a staff member would mimic or be effected by it. You could call memetic effect here but there's no other support for that, methinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is far from bad, it just needs polish. Most of the issues I mention below are grammar or vocabulary based (other users are covering the rest). The amount of Russian (and the cyrillic alphabet) being used is probably a stylistic thing, but I get the feeling that some of this has been translated (wild guess, but a handful of words/phrases mistranslating would explain most of these issues).

+ show block
– hide block

Mobile Task Force Sigma-3

I think this is a typo. It's the only mention of this task force.

nomadistic

nomadic

EDIT: Googled it after the fact. Nomadistic may be correct depending on context, but every spell check I try says it's wrong. Most of what I see on the academic end is nomadic (with nomadistic being used occasionally in translations), and nomadistic seems to mostly be used by those who don't know the term nomadic and as a username by at least one person.

capable of communication through speech in varying dialects

This may just be an issue with my vocabulary, but I was under the impression that dialects were like regional variations of the same language. While the footnote addresses what language/dialects are typically used, this seems like it should be worded differently with the footnote handling the specifics (it kind of does already).

Progress Engineering

Foundation shells typically end up with the acronym SCP, and those in other languages either have that acronym or take it on when translated into English. I don't know if this is enforced or just what most authors default to as an in-joke, but I figured I'd bring it up. It isn't affecting my vote.

Interview Excerpt: SCP-2595-399

I was going to bring up some grammar issues in this, but then I realized that most of them may be intentional for stylistic reasons. If this was intentional, good job.

That being said, there are some from the English speaking researcher that are a little off.

You do seem to understand me. Does the usage of English make you uncomfortable?

Drop the "do" in the first sentence and change "usage" to use.

I do think that it is clear for your species

Similar issue with the "do" here. I've rarely seen it used in this manner except where emphasis was desired.

I am sure i will have plenty of time to be remembered by the fellows of my kind due to a superior contribution. Unfortunately, i do not seem to be able to foresee the same future within your empty prophesying.

This is the doctor slipping into what I thought was a stylistic choice being used by 399. Specifically, the lack of "I" capitalization. While this could be a method of indicating something about the manner in which 399 speaks, a native English speaker would likely not have the same speech peculiarities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand where you were trying to go, but what is being mentioned here is that the doctor's tone is not natural. If you are a scientist/doctor/researcher trying to interview someone, it would be within your best interests to remain precise, clinical, and respectful. In other words, this doctor has no reason to have an emotional response to some anomalous being he/she is interviewing for the first time. Not only does it sound odd (the interview), but it sounds as if the instance and doctor are having some beef we don't know about.

If this instance willingly turned itself in, then it should provide answers (or at the very least provide a hint of some sort). Similarly, the doctor is expected to question the instance normally.

It seems that you are also inclined to explain your intentions behind what you wrote and why in the comments. This is good. However, whatever emotional response you are trying to elicit is poorly defined under a random addendum and stilted interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
four-leveled hierarchical pattern.

Yet then only 3 are described?
Wouldn't that work against the 'hide this information' if the 4th group is the D statues?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I catch your point and i will make some tentative changes to the interview in order to get the tone right as soon as i get back to my computer, which should be rather soon. Thank you for your input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess where the dialog falters is that the entity doesn't really sound particularly threatening. His tone strikes me more like Pedantic College Professor Who Knows More About This Topic Than You But Is Overall Not That Smart.

Have you read the dialog aloud? Both characters are talking to each other but it doesn't have much natural flow from them both generally not answering/responding directly to each other.

+ show block
– hide block

Dr. ████████: You do seem to understand me. Does the usage of English make you uncomfortable?

SCP-2595-399: It's a matter of perspective, doctor. If i recall correctly, your modern vocabulary was structured by a language quite similar to the one i use.

This is a case of the entity being more out-of-step than the doctor but overall it's still representative of the issue at hand. Also as a grammatical concern, your pronoun "I" is missing capitalization in a few places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to clarify that he is talking to a common member of the anomalous community. The interviewed guy is not a godhead, but he does recognize that they hold very strong capabilities.

The tone of the doctor gets less clinical as he attempts to match the esoteric "we will destroy you" intimidating talk. However, as you read it, you start to see how much is he failing.

Edit: The interview has been remade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, that is indeed a typo that i intend to correct as soon as i get my hands on my computer again.

The whole point of the interview is to show that the community is not absolutely passive. The formal English tone used by the entity shows that the overall intelligence among the society is u.nusually high. The second point of the interview is to show that they actually know that they have the ability to turn a whole continent into a mass of anomalous plastic humanoids.

The bravado and the tone of the doctor gradually increase as a response to the intimidation done by the interviewed entity. He is basically attempting to go toe to toe and failing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this immensely until I opened the collapsible.

I find all the stuff about 2595-D to be far less clearly described than A-C. I also found the interview to be decidedly odd. Both the creature and the doctor spoke as if English was their second language. I couldn't tell if this was intentional. Furthermore, I found the doctor to be very confrontational and unprofessional.

"Dr. ████████: I am sure i will have plenty of time to be remembered by the fellows of my kind due to a superior contribution. Unfortunately, i do not seem to be able to foresee the same future within your empty prophesying."

I don't feel like telling a perhaps deity that its prophecies are "empty" is very clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This class serves a purpose similar to that of a "council", aiding SCP-2595-D…

Is this supposed to be 2595-B?

Overall this is well-written, although I think the doctor's dialog in the interview is a little loose for my tastes; he doesn't push very strongly for the information he desires, and the bit of bravado at the end about being "remembered by the fellows of my kind due to a superior contribution" isn't great tone. The entire interview is really a bit odd; they seem to talk around each other more than anything. This is a no-vote for me right now but is closer to up than down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an idle thought, I often wonder why the moniker of 'scruffy', whilst nominally a descriptive term, tends to get applied so frequently here.

Must merely be coincidental.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here we go. I hope this one does well.

A very special thank you to everyone who revised it in chat, including:

Nach0z
Tagliafierro
AndarielHalo
TwistedGears
Athena_Grey
Nikolaus
Bunton

Enjoy your read!
